title: The Feast-and-Famine Cycle and How to Widen the Floor
subtitle: A reading on the income pattern most common among self-employed people and what breaks it
excerpt: A reading on the feast-and-famine income cycle common among self-employed and freelance workers. Explains why the ceiling matters less than the floor, what keeps the cycle in place, and what the inner work is that actually widens the floor.

practice.whenToUse: Read if your income is inconsistent, with good months and then unexpectedly quiet ones — and especially if the pattern keeps repeating even as your business grows.

content.text: The feast-and-famine cycle is one of the most common patterns among self-employed people. A good month, then a slow one. A launch that works, then a lull. The cycle tends to persist regardless of how experienced or established the business is, because it's not primarily a marketing or sales problem.
What keeps the cycle in place

Two internal patterns sustain the cycle. The first is the ceiling: an internal cap on how much income feels safe or allowed. When income approaches that ceiling, the energy around marketing and sales unconsciously pulls back. Something always seems to go wrong at the same level.

The second is the floor: the lowest income level the person has tolerated and recovered from. When income drops back to the floor, action restarts. The crisis motivates. The floor is learned. It doesn't rise on its own.
Why raising the ceiling isn't the goal

Most people try to fix the cycle by chasing a higher ceiling: a better launch, a bigger audience, more offers. This can work temporarily, but the pattern reasserts. The ceiling raises slightly, then income drops back proportionally. The swing just gets bigger.

The work that actually breaks the cycle is raising the floor. This means identifying the lowest income you're unconsciously willing to tolerate, and doing the inner work to make that level unacceptable as the new normal. Not through discipline or willpower, but through genuine identity shift. When the floor rises, the swings narrow.
What the inner work looks like

The feast-and-famine pattern often has a strong identity component: the belief that inconsistent income is just what self-employment looks like, or that you're not the type of person who has steady income. Releasing those beliefs and building the identity of someone with a consistent floor is the direction of the work.
The MONEY v2 arc addresses this directly in the Phase 2 identity work. The practices in the overflow and abundance sections work on it from the top down.
